commands.repair_reason: - [P1] Needs contributor-owned real startup proof and maintainer coordination with the overlapping startup PR rather than an automated code repair.; Cleared: The diff only changes ACP startup reconcile logic and a focused unit test; no concrete security or supply-chain concern was found. (sha=77dc32a106fd6d72fc9d05c7da5d1a36b1b88618)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a `mode === "oneshot"` guard to ACP startup identity reconciliation and a regression test that asserts one-shot pending identities do not call runtime ensure/status during startup.

PR surface: Source +3, Tests +40. Total +43 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source. A persisted one-shot ACP meta with `identity.state: "pending"` and a stable `acpxSessionId` reaches the current startup ensure/status path; I did not run a live gateway restart reproduction.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Needs contributor-owned real startup proof and maintainer coordination with the overlapping startup PR rather than an automated code repair.

target.title: fix(acp): skip oneshot sessions during startup identity reconcile
target.body:
## Summary

Gateway startup identity reconciliation attempts to resolve pending ACP session identities. Oneshot sessions are terminal by nature, but their identity often remains `pending` after completion. The reconcile loop tries to ensure a runtime handle for each one, which fails and produces misleading warnings:

```text
[gateway] acp startup identity reconcile (renderer=v1): checked=7 resolved=0 failed=7
```

This makes it look like ACP/Codex/Claude agent routing is broken when fresh sessions are healthy.

## Fix

Skip sessions with `mode === "oneshot"` in `runManagerStartupIdentityReconcile` so the startup scan only processes persistent sessions that may genuinely need identity resolution.

## Changed files

- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.startup-identity-reconcile.ts` -- Added `session.acp.mode === "oneshot"` guard after the session key check
- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.startup-identity-reconcile.test.ts` -- Regression test: oneshot session with pending identity is skipped (checked=0, no ensure/getStatus calls)

## Overlap with #85399

PR #85399 (bound startup sidecar fanout) rewrites the same startup reconcile loop. This PR's guard (`session.acp.mode === "oneshot" -> continue`) is an independent behavior concern from #85399's fanout bounding. Both changes apply cleanly on the same function. Landing order should not matter as long as the guard is preserved during any rebase.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ACP startup identity reconciliation processes oneshot sessions unnecessarily, producing misleading "checked=7 resolved=0 failed=7" warnings and making ensure calls for ephemeral sessions.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw built from patched source.

The oneshot guard is compiled into the AcpSessionManager production bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "oneshot.*continue\|reconcilePendingSessionIdentities" dist/manager-*.js
753:async reconcilePendingSessionIdentities(params) {
770:if (session.acp.mode === "oneshot") continue;
```

Line 770 shows the guard that skips oneshot sessions during startup identity reconciliation.

Node runtime exercise of the patched reconciliation logic with a mix of oneshot and interactive sessions:

```console
  SKIP oneshot: session-1
  CHECK: session-2 (mode=interactive, identity=pending)
  SKIP oneshot: session-3
  SKIP resolved: session-4

Result: checked=1, skippedOneshot=2
Before fix: all 4 sessions checked, 2 oneshot failures in startup log
After fix: only 1 interactive-pending session checked, 2 oneshot sessions skipped
```

Observed result after fix: The `mode === "oneshot"` guard causes `runManagerStartupIdentityReconcile` to skip oneshot sessions entirely. Only interactive sessions with pending identities trigger ensure calls. Eliminates misleading warning output on gateway startup.

What was not tested: Live ACP runtime with real persisted stale oneshot sessions (requires active ACP backend with historical Codex session data). Redacted gateway startup logs would confirm the warning disappears in production.
